metadata.subject: Career Education
metadata.description: Career Education 9 deepens students' self-awareness and career exploration, building on Grade 8 foundations. Students refine their understanding of their own strengths and interests, explore career clusters and pathways more specifically, develop decision-making frameworks, and begin planning their secondary school journey more intentionally. Connections to community, First Peoples perspectives on work, and practical workplace readiness skills are central. This course prepares students for the career planning process that culminates in the Graduation Transitions requirement.
metadata.general_description: A career education course deepening self-awareness, career exploration, decision-making, community connection, and secondary school planning — building toward the Graduation Transitions requirement and post-secondary readiness.

big_ideas.statement: Reflecting on our preferences and skills helps us identify the steps we need to take to achieve our career goals.
big_ideas.elaboration: Deepening self-knowledge — understanding not just what we are good at but why certain work feels meaningful — enables more intentional and satisfying career planning at every stage of life.

big_ideas.statement: The value of work in our lives, communities, and society can be viewed from diverse perspectives.
big_ideas.elaboration: As students encounter a wider range of people and communities, they develop richer understanding of how work is defined, valued, and organized across cultures — including Indigenous communities, local industries, and global contexts.

big_ideas.statement: Achieving our learning goals requires effort and perseverance.
big_ideas.elaboration: Pursuing meaningful goals — academically, personally, and professionally — requires the capacity to sustain effort through difficulty and to learn from setbacks. Resilience is a skill that can be developed.

big_ideas.statement: Adapting to economic and labour market changes requires flexibility.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students entering the workforce will face labour markets shaped by automation, climate change, and globalization. Developing transferable skills and a mindset of lifelong learning prepares them for this reality.

big_ideas.statement: Our career paths reflect the personal, community, and educational choices we make.
big_ideas.elaboration: As students approach senior secondary, the relationship between their current choices — courses, activities, relationships, values — and their future pathways becomes increasingly concrete and significant.

competencies.raw: Use self-assessment and reflection to develop awareness of strengths, preferences, and skills; recognize the impact of personal public identity in the workplace; demonstrate respect, collaboration, and inclusivity when solving problems; set and achieve realistic learning goals with perseverance and resilience.
i_can_statements: I can use self-assessment tools and honest reflection to deepen my understanding of my strengths, preferences, and skills.
I can recognize how my public identity — including online presence — affects how I am perceived in workplace and community settings.
I can demonstrate respect, collaboration, and inclusivity when working with diverse people and perspectives.
I can set meaningful learning goals, develop plans to achieve them, and show resilience when I encounter setbacks.

competencies.raw: Question how individual purposes and passions can support community needs when considering career choices; explore diverse perspectives on how work contributes to community and society; appreciate the value of mentors and resource networks; apply research skills to expand knowledge of diverse career possibilities and clusters.
i_can_statements: I can critically examine how my passions and strengths connect to community needs and broader social purposes.
I can explore diverse perspectives on work — including First Peoples, multicultural, and global perspectives — with curiosity and respect.
I can identify and build relationships with mentors and community members who can support my career exploration.
I can apply increasingly sophisticated research skills to explore career options, post-secondary pathways, and labour market trends.

competencies.raw: Recognize how curriculum choices influence career paths; question the role of family expectations and community needs in career choices; explore volunteer experiences and new learning that stimulate entrepreneurial and innovative thinking; apply decision-making strategies and adjust them to new situations; demonstrate safety skills and appreciate workplace safety.
i_can_statements: I can reflect on how my course selections and learning experiences are shaping my future options.
I can thoughtfully question and balance family expectations, community needs, and my own values in career planning.
I can explore entrepreneurship and innovation through new experiences, volunteering, and creative problem-solving.
I can apply and adjust decision-making strategies as my circumstances and priorities evolve.
I can demonstrate workplace safety knowledge including hazard identification, WHMIS, and worker rights and responsibilities.

areas.name: Personal Development
areas.raw: Goal-setting, self-assessment, reflection, and project management.
topics: Deepened self-assessment: personality inventories (e.g., Holland codes, Myers-Briggs overview), skills profiles
Identifying transferable skills: communication, problem-solving, collaboration, and adaptability
Values clarification: identifying non-negotiables and priorities in future work
Resilience and growth mindset: strategies for persisting through academic and personal challenges
Project-based learning: planning, executing, and reflecting on a sustained personal or community project
Time management and organization: practical strategies for balancing competing demands

areas.name: Connections to Community
areas.raw: Local and global needs, cultural awareness, volunteering, and career clusters.
topics: Career clusters in depth: exploring specific careers within 1-2 clusters of personal interest
Labour market information: current and emerging occupations in BC and Canada
Global career trends: automation, green economy, health care, and technology sectors
First Peoples economies: traditional and contemporary Indigenous business, land management, and community work
Community connection: job shadowing, informational interviews, and community service learning
Entrepreneurship: exploring social entrepreneurship and innovation in local communities
Financial literacy and work: wages, budgeting, taxes, and the relationship between education and earning

areas.name: Life and Career Plan
areas.raw: Graduation planning, mentors, technology, workplace safety, and career planning resources.
topics: Grade 9-12 course planning: mapping courses to graduation requirements and post-secondary goals
Secondary school pathways: academic, trades, arts, and dual credit options
Post-secondary overview: college, university, trades/apprenticeship, and work options
Workplace safety: WHMIS 2015, young worker safety, and BC employment standards
Technology and the future of work: AI, remote work, and evolving skill requirements
Developing a personal career portfolio: collecting evidence of skills and achievements
Introduction to Graduation Transitions: requirements and purpose
